paper_title: Adaptive Load Balancing in Microservice Architectures: A Machine Learning Approach
affiliation: Department of Computer Science, Stanford University
abstract: This paper presents a novel adaptive load balancing algorithm for microservice architectures that leverages reinforcement learning to dynamically distribute traffic based on real-time service health metrics. We evaluate our approach against traditional round-robin and least-connections strategies across three production-scale workloads. Results demonstrate a 34% reduction in p99 latency and a 28% improvement in throughput under high-contention scenarios, while maintaining comparable performance under low-load conditions.
keywords: load balancing, microservices, reinforcement learning, distributed systems, cloud computing
introduction:
The proliferation of microservice architectures has introduced new challenges in traffic management and resource allocation. Traditional load balancing strategies such as round-robin and least-connections fail to account for the heterogeneous nature of modern microservice deployments, where individual services exhibit varying latency profiles, resource consumption patterns, and failure modes.

Recent advances in machine learning, particularly reinforcement learning (RL), offer promising approaches to adaptive decision-making in dynamic environments. In this paper, we propose AdaptLB, a reinforcement learning-based load balancer that continuously learns optimal routing policies from real-time telemetry data.
methodology:
Our experimental setup consists of a 48-node Kubernetes cluster running a representative e-commerce microservice application with 12 distinct services. We instrument each service with Prometheus metrics exporters collecting latency, error rate, CPU utilisation, and memory consumption at 5-second intervals.

The AdaptLB agent is implemented as a Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO) model trained on a state space comprising normalised metrics from all downstream service instances. The action space represents routing weight distributions across available replicas. We train the agent using 72 hours of production traffic replay data before deploying to the live cluster.
results:
Table 1 summarises our key findings across three workload profiles. Under the high-contention workload (simulating Black Friday traffic patterns), AdaptLB achieved a p99 latency of 142ms compared to 214ms for round-robin and 189ms for least-connections — a 34% and 25% improvement respectively.

Throughput under the same conditions increased from 12,400 req/s (round-robin) to 15,900 req/s (AdaptLB), representing a 28% improvement. Notably, AdaptLB maintained these gains even as we introduced artificial fault injection, demonstrating robust performance degradation characteristics.
conclusion:
We have demonstrated that reinforcement learning-based load balancing can significantly outperform traditional strategies in microservice environments, particularly under high-contention and fault-injection scenarios. The key contribution of this work is showing that a PPO-based agent can learn effective routing policies from standard observability metrics without requiring application-level instrumentation.

Future work will explore multi-cluster federation, where a hierarchical RL approach may further improve global traffic management across geographically distributed deployments.
references:
[1] Burns, B. et al. "Borg, Omega, and Kubernetes." ACM Queue, 14(1), 2016.
[2] Mao, H. et al. "Resource Management with Deep Reinforcement Learning." HotNets, 2016.
[3] Schulman, J. et al. "Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms." arXiv:1707.06347, 2017.
[4] Gan, Y. et al. "An Open-Source Benchmark Suite for Microservices." ASPLOS, 2019.
[5] Qiu, H. et al. "FIRM: An Intelligent Fine-Grained Resource Management Framework." OSDI, 2020.
